prophecies.title: The Protoevangelium — Seed of the Woman
prophecies.text_snippet: He shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel

christian_view.explanation: Christians read this as the first messianic prophecy (the 'protoevangelium'). The 'seed of the woman' is Christ, born of a virgin (Gal 4:4), who crushes Satan's authority through the cross and resurrection. The 'bruising of the heel' represents Christ's suffering and death; the 'crushing of the head' is Satan's decisive defeat (Col 2:15). The phrase 'seed of the woman' is unique — seed is normally reckoned through the father in Hebrew genealogy — hinting at the virgin birth.

jewish_view.explanation: Orthodox Judaism generally does not read Genesis 3:15 as a messianic prophecy. The mainstream rabbinic reading (Rashi, Ibn Ezra) treats it as an etiological explanation for the enmity between humans and snakes — humans step on snakes' heads, snakes bite at human heels. At most, some see a general promise that humanity will eventually overcome evil. The Targum Pseudo-Jonathan does add 'in the days of King Messiah,' but this is a late Aramaic paraphrase and not representative of mainstream rabbinic exegesis. The passage does not appear in the major messianic proof-text lists of the Talmud.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The Septuagint (LXX) translates the pronoun as masculine 'autos' (he) rather than the expected neuter 'auto' (it, to agree with 'sperma'/seed), suggesting the Alexandrian translators (~250 BC) saw an individual figure. The Hebrew 'hu' is grammatically masculine but could refer to the collective 'seed.' The Vulgate's 'ipsa' (she) — reading the woman herself as the crusher — is almost certainly a scribal error but influenced centuries of Catholic Marian interpretation. The verb 'shuph' (bruise/crush/strike) is the same for both parties, though the target differs (head vs. heel), implying asymmetric outcomes.

prophecies.title: The Abrahamic Covenant — All Nations Blessed
prophecies.text_snippet: In you all the families of the earth shall be blessed... in your seed all the nations of the earth shall be blessed

christian_view.explanation: Paul argues in Galatians 3:16 that the singular 'seed' (zera) points to one descendant: Christ. Through Christ, the blessing of Abraham extends to all nations (Gentiles included) by faith. The land promise is spiritualized in Hebrews (11:10, 16) as pointing to a heavenly city. The covenant of circumcision is fulfilled in 'circumcision of the heart' (Rom 2:29; Col 2:11). Acts 3:25-26 explicitly connects the Abrahamic promise to Jesus.

jewish_view.explanation: Judaism affirms the Abrahamic covenant as foundational and eternal. The 'seed' (zera) refers to Abraham's physical descendants — the Jewish people collectively, not a single individual. The blessing to 'all families of the earth' is understood as coming through Israel's role as a 'light to the nations' (Isa 42:6) — the nations are blessed because Israel preserves monotheism, Torah, and moral law. The land promise (Gen 15:18-21) is literal and pertains to the physical land of Israel, partially fulfilled under Joshua and David, fully awaited in the messianic age. Circumcision remains a literal physical covenant obligation (Gen 17:10-14). Maimonides (Hilkhot Melakhim 11:1) sees the full realization as future.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The Hebrew 'zera' is a collective noun (like English 'offspring') that can be singular or plural depending on context. Paul's argument in Galatians 3:16 about the singular form is a midrashic reading — grammatically, 'zera' is always morphologically singular even when meaning 'descendants' (cf. Gen 22:17 where 'zera' clearly means many). The LXX uses 'sperma' (also collective). The tension between literal land promises and spiritual fulfillment remains one of the oldest hermeneutical debates in biblical theology. The Abrahamic covenant is 'unconditional' (no 'if' clauses in Gen 15), unlike the Mosaic covenant.

prophecies.title: The Shiloh Prophecy — Scepter of Judah
prophecies.text_snippet: The scepter shall not depart from Judah... until Shiloh comes, and to him shall be the obedience of the peoples

christian_view.explanation: Christians identify 'Shiloh' as a messianic title. Jesus was born of the tribe of Judah (Matt 1:2-3; Heb 7:14). The prophecy predicts that Judah's tribal authority would persist 'until Shiloh comes,' and the scepter departed from Judah around the time of Christ (Herod, an Idumean, held the throne; Judean self-governance ended). The phrase 'obedience of the peoples' points to the Gentile nations submitting to Christ. Revelation 5:5 calls Jesus 'the Lion of the tribe of Judah.'

jewish_view.explanation: This is recognized as a messianic prophecy in rabbinic tradition — the Talmud (Sanhedrin 98b) discusses it, and Targum Onkelos translates 'until Messiah comes, whose is the kingdom.' However, the identity of Messiah is the issue: Judaism awaits a future Davidic king who will restore Israel's sovereignty, rebuild the Temple, and bring world peace. The scepter's 'departure' from Judah does not prove Jesus was the Messiah — rather, it indicates the messianic era has not yet arrived, since these conditions remain unfulfilled. Some interpret 'Shiloh' as 'he to whom it belongs' (shello) or as a reference to the city Shiloh, not as a personal messianic name.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The word 'Shiloh' (shiloh) is one of the most debated terms in the Hebrew Bible. Options: (1) A proper messianic name; (2) 'he to whom it belongs' (shello, cf. Ezek 21:27); (3) a reference to the city of Shiloh (where the tabernacle rested); (4) from shalah, 'to be tranquil' — 'the tranquil one.' The LXX reads 'ta apokeimena auto' (the things stored up for him), supporting reading (2). The Qumran text 4Q252 (Patriarchal Blessings) reads this passage messianically: 'until the Messiah of Righteousness comes, the Branch of David.' This confirms that pre-Christian Judaism read Gen 49:10 as messianic.

prophecies.title: The Binding of Isaac (Aqedah) — Substitutionary Sacrifice
prophecies.text_snippet: God will provide for Himself the lamb for the burnt offering, my son

christian_view.explanation: Christians see the Aqedah as the supreme typological foreshadowing of the cross. Abraham offered his 'only son, whom you love' (Gen 22:2) on Mount Moriah — traditionally identified with the Temple Mount / Golgotha. Isaac carried the wood up the mountain as Christ carried the cross. God provided a ram as a substitute, foreshadowing the Lamb of God (John 1:29). Hebrews 11:19 says Abraham received Isaac back 'as a type' (en parabole). The phrase 'God will provide' (YHWH yireh) is prophetic — God did provide the ultimate sacrifice on that same mountain.

jewish_view.explanation: The Aqedah is profoundly important in Judaism but is not read as pointing to Jesus. It demonstrates Abraham's ultimate faithfulness and Isaac's willing submission (rabbinic tradition emphasizes Isaac was not a child but a grown man who consented). The merit of the Aqedah (zekhut Avot) is invoked in Jewish liturgy — particularly in the Rosh Hashanah prayers — as ongoing merit for Israel before God. The shofar (ram's horn) blown on Rosh Hashanah recalls the ram substituted for Isaac. The Aqedah teaches that God does NOT desire human sacrifice — the opposite of how Christians apply it. It is about the rejection of child sacrifice prevalent in Canaan, not a foreshadowing of divine sacrifice.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The location 'land of Moriah' (Gen 22:2) is linked to the Temple Mount by 2 Chronicles 3:1 but this identification is debated. The Samaritan tradition places it on Mount Gerizim. The age of Isaac is not stated — Jewish tradition (Josephus, Genesis Rabbah) says 25 or 37; Christian tradition often depicts him as a youth. The narrative's theological function differs sharply between traditions: Christianity sees foreshadowing of substitutionary atonement; Judaism sees the establishment of a merit principle and the end of human sacrifice in Israelite religion. Both agree on the text; the interpretive framework differs fundamentally.

prophecies.title: Proto-Sacrificial Pattern — Blood Covering and Priestly Mediation
prophecies.text_snippet: The LORD God made garments of skin for Adam and his wife... Melchizedek king of Salem brought out bread and wine; he was priest of God Most High

christian_view.explanation: Christians trace a sacrificial thread through Genesis: God provides animal skins (requiring death) to cover Adam and Eve's nakedness/shame (3:21). Abel's accepted offering involves blood (4:4). Noah's sacrifice produces a 'soothing aroma' to the LORD (8:20-21). Melchizedek (14:18-20), a priest-king with no recorded genealogy, foreshadows Christ's eternal priesthood (Heb 7). The bread and wine of Melchizedek prefigure the Eucharist. Psalm 110:4 explicitly links the Messiah to Melchizedek's priestly order, which Hebrews develops at length.

jewish_view.explanation: Judaism acknowledges these sacrificial narratives but does not read them as pointing to a future atoning death of the Messiah. Animal sacrifice is understood within its ANE context and later codified in Torah. Melchizedek is identified in some rabbinic traditions (Genesis Rabbah 43:6; Bavli Nedarim 32b) as Shem, son of Noah, whose priesthood was transferred to Abraham's line because Melchizedek blessed Abraham before blessing God. The Levitical priesthood superseded Melchizedek's order. There is no concept of a 'priest-king Messiah' combining both offices in mainstream rabbinic theology — Messiah is a Davidic king, not a priest (priests are from Levi, kings from Judah).

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The Melchizedek figure appears in the Dead Sea Scrolls (11QMelchizedek / 11Q13) as an exalted heavenly figure who executes divine judgment — significantly, he is identified with the 'elohim' of Psalm 82:1 and the 'messenger' of Isaiah 52:7. This pre-Christian Jewish text shows that a divine/exalted Melchizedek figure was already part of Second Temple Judaism before Christianity developed its Christology. The Qumran community apparently saw Melchizedek as more than a historical Canaanite priest-king. The dual office of priest-king in one person was unusual in Israelite tradition (cf. Zech 6:12-13 where the Branch 'shall be a priest on his throne').

prophecies.title: The Prophet Like Moses
prophecies.text_snippet: The LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your brothers — it is to him you shall listen

christian_view.explanation: Peter explicitly identifies Jesus as the 'prophet like Moses' in Acts 3:22-23, quoting Deuteronomy 18 directly. Stephen repeats the identification in Acts 7:37. The crowds in John 6:14 recognize Jesus as 'the Prophet who is to come into the world' after the feeding of the 5,000 (paralleling Moses and manna). Jesus is like Moses but greater: Moses mediated the old covenant, Jesus mediates the new; Moses led Israel out of Egypt, Jesus leads humanity out of bondage to sin; Moses spoke with God 'face to face,' Jesus IS God incarnate (John 1:14, 18).

jewish_view.explanation: Rabbinic tradition has two main readings. (1) This refers to the prophetic office in general — God will raise up prophets throughout Israel's history (Joshua, Samuel, Elijah, etc.) as successors to Moses, and the passage sets criteria for true vs. false prophets (vv. 20-22). This is Rashi's position. (2) It refers to a specific future prophet — the Messiah — who will be 'like Moses' in authority and intimacy with God. Maimonides (Mishneh Torah, Yesodei HaTorah 7:6) holds that the future Messiah will be a prophet of this stature. Either way, this prophet has not yet come in Jewish theology, because the messianic conditions (peace, Temple, ingathering) are unfulfilled.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The Samaritan community based their entire messianic expectation (the 'Taheb' — the Restorer) on this passage, not on the Davidic promises. In the DSS, the community at Qumran expected THREE eschatological figures: the Prophet (Deut 18), the Messiah of Israel (Davidic king), and the Messiah of Aaron (priestly figure) — see 1QS 9:11. This shows that Second Temple Judaism could distinguish the 'prophet like Moses' from the Davidic Messiah. The woman at the well in John 4:19, 25 reflects the Samaritan Taheb expectation. The phrase 'from among your brothers' limits this to an Israelite — not an angel or a foreign figure.

prophecies.title: Blessings, Curses, and Future Restoration
prophecies.text_snippet: The LORD will scatter you among all peoples, from one end of the earth to the other... then the LORD your God will restore your fortunes and have compassion on you, and He will gather you again from all the peoples

christian_view.explanation: Christians see the curses of Deuteronomy 28 as fulfilled in the Babylonian exile (586 BC) and again — with devastating precision — in the Roman destruction of Jerusalem (70 AD) and the subsequent diaspora. The horrific details of 28:49-57 (a nation 'from far away, like the eagle swoops,' siege cannibalism) match Josephus's account of 70 AD. The restoration promises of chapter 30 are debated among Christians: some see them fulfilled spiritually in the Church; others (premillennialists) see a future literal restoration of Israel in conjunction with Christ's return (Rom 11:25-27 — 'all Israel will be saved').

jewish_view.explanation: Judaism sees the curses as tragically fulfilled multiple times — the Babylonian exile, the Roman destruction, and the centuries of diaspora persecution. The modern State of Israel (1948) is seen by many religious Jews as the beginning of the ingathering promised in Deut 30:3-5, though opinion varies: Religious Zionism sees it as 'the beginning of the flowering of redemption' (atchalta d'geulah); ultra-Orthodox groups (Satmar, Neturei Karta) argue the true ingathering can only come through the Messiah, not through a secular state. The 'circumcision of the heart' (30:6) is understood as something God will do in the messianic age to enable perfect Torah observance.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Deuteronomy 28-30 follows the structure of ancient Near Eastern suzerainty treaties (notably the Vassal Treaties of Esarhaddon, 672 BC), where blessings and curses are standard concluding elements. Some scholars argue chapters 28-30 were composed or heavily edited after 586 BC as vaticinium ex eventu (prophecy after the fact). Conservative scholars counter that the treaty form was already well-established in the Late Bronze Age (Hittite treaties, 14th-13th c. BC), making a Mosaic-era composition plausible. Deut 30:6 ('circumcise your heart') anticipates Jeremiah 31:31-34 (the new covenant) and Ezekiel 36:26 (new heart).

prophecies.title: The Song of Moses — Judgment and Vindication
prophecies.text_snippet: Rejoice, O nations, with His people; for He will avenge the blood of His servants... and will atone for His land and His people

christian_view.explanation: Paul quotes Deut 32:21 in Romans 10:19 (Israel provoked to jealousy by Gentile inclusion) and 32:35 in Romans 12:19 ('Vengeance is Mine, says the LORD'). Hebrews 1:6 may allude to 32:43 (LXX: 'let all the angels of God worship him'). Revelation 15:3-4 echoes the Song of Moses as the eschatological victory hymn. Christians see the Song's narrative arc — Israel's apostasy, God's judgment, then God's ultimate vindication of His people — as the pattern of redemptive history fulfilled in Christ and consummated at His return.

jewish_view.explanation: The Song of Moses (Ha'azinu) is one of the most important liturgical texts in Judaism. It is read as the Torah portion for Shabbat Shuvah (between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur). The Talmud (Sanhedrin 91b) derives the doctrine of resurrection from 32:39 ('I kill and I make alive'). The Song traces Israel's entire history: election, apostasy, punishment, and ultimate vindication. The final verse (32:43) promises that God will avenge Israel's enemies and 'atone for His land and His people' — this is understood as eschatological, awaiting fulfillment in the messianic age.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The textual tradition of Deut 32:8-9 is significant: the Masoretic Text reads 'according to the number of the sons of Israel' (bene yisrael), but the LXX reads 'according to the number of the angels of God' (angelon theou), and 4QDeut-j from Qumran reads 'sons of God' (bene elohim). The Qumran/LXX reading implies a divine council theology: God assigned nations to lesser divine beings but kept Israel for Himself (v.9). This reading is now preferred by most text critics and dramatically affects the theology of the passage. Verse 32:43 also differs significantly between MT, LXX, and 4QDeut-32 — the Qumran version is longer and includes 'heavens' and 'gods' bowing to YHWH.

prophecies.title: The Son's Enthronement — Kiss the Son
prophecies.text_snippet: You are My Son; today I have begotten You. Ask of Me, and I will give You the nations as Your inheritance

christian_view.explanation: The early church directly applied Psalm 2 to Jesus in Acts 4:25-28, identifying Herod and Pilate as the 'kings' who conspired against the LORD's Anointed. Acts 13:33 and Hebrews 1:5 apply 'You are My Son' to Christ's resurrection and exaltation. The 'begetting' is understood not as a temporal origin but as enthronement — the moment of public declaration (cf. the baptism and transfiguration, where the Father says 'This is My beloved Son'). The 'rod of iron' rule over nations (2:9) awaits final fulfillment at the Second Coming (Rev 19:15).

jewish_view.explanation: Psalm 2 is understood as a royal psalm originally composed for the coronation of a Davidic king — possibly Solomon or a later Judahite king. The 'son' language (v.7) reflects the covenant of 2 Samuel 7:14 where God adopts the Davidic king as His 'son.' This is adoptive sonship, not ontological deity. Rabbinic tradition (Talmud Sukkah 52a; Berakhot 7b) does read this psalm messianically — it refers to the future Messiah who will rule the nations. But this Messiah is a human king, not a divine being. The psalm awaits fulfillment because no Davidic king has yet ruled all nations in peace. Rashi applies 'my son' to David himself or to Israel collectively.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Psalm 2 is a royal psalm of the Jerusalem court, likely pre-exilic. The 'begetting' formula ('You are My Son, today I have begotten You') parallels Egyptian coronation texts where Pharaoh is declared 'son of Re' at enthronement. In Israel, this was adoptive/covenantal, not mythological. The word 'mashiach' (anointed one) in v.2 originally referred to any anointed king, but by the Second Temple period had acquired eschatological meaning. The last verse is textually uncertain: 'kiss the son' (nashqu bar) uses Aramaic 'bar' for 'son' instead of Hebrew 'ben' — some emend to 'nashqu berakhah' (kiss sincerely/with purity) or see it as an Aramaism. The LXX reads 'draxasthe paideias' (embrace discipline).

prophecies.title: Resurrection — You Will Not Abandon My Soul to Sheol
prophecies.text_snippet: You will not abandon my soul to Sheol, nor will You let Your Holy One see corruption. You will make known to me the path of life

christian_view.explanation: Peter's Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:24-32) makes Psalm 16 the centerpiece of the first Christian proclamation of the resurrection. Peter's argument: David wrote this psalm, but David DID die and his tomb is 'with us to this day' (v.29). Therefore David was not speaking about himself but prophetically about the Messiah, whose body did not see decay because God raised Him from the dead. Paul repeats this argument in Acts 13:35-37. The key word is 'shachat' (corruption/pit/decay) — Christ's body was in the tomb but did not decompose.

jewish_view.explanation: The traditional Jewish reading (Rashi, Radak/Kimchi) understands Psalm 16 as David's personal prayer of confidence. David is expressing trust that God will protect him from premature death and the grave — not that he will be physically resurrected. 'You will not abandon my soul to Sheol' means God will preserve David's life in his current crisis. 'Your Holy One' (chasidekha) refers to David himself (as God's faithful one), not to a future Messiah. The psalm is about trust in God's protection in this life, not about resurrection from the dead. Radak explicitly argues against the Christian reading.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The critical word is 'shachat' — it can mean either 'pit' (a synonym for Sheol) or 'corruption/decay.' The LXX translated it as 'diaphthora' (corruption/decay), which is the reading Acts relies on. If the meaning is 'pit,' the psalm simply says 'you will not let me go down to the pit' (i.e., you will preserve my life). If 'corruption,' it implies the body will not decay — i.e., resurrection. The Masoretic vocalization (shahat) favors 'pit,' while the LXX reading favors 'corruption.' The Ketiv-Qere notes in the MT show the tradition preserved both. The psalm's superscription is 'miktam of David,' a genre term of uncertain meaning.

prophecies.title: The Suffering of the Righteous One
prophecies.text_snippet: My God, my God, why have You forsaken me?... They pierced my hands and my feet... they divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots

christian_view.explanation: Psalm 22 is the most detailed prophetic description of crucifixion in the OT, written centuries before crucifixion was invented as a method of execution. Jesus quoted the opening line from the cross (Matt 27:46). The psalm describes: mocking crowds (v.7-8 / Matt 27:39, 43), hands and feet pierced (v.16 / John 20:25), bones out of joint (v.14 / dislocation on the cross), garments divided and lots cast (v.18 / John 19:23-24), dehydration (v.15 / John 19:28), and public shame (v.6-8 / Matt 27:39-44). The psalm then pivots to triumph (vv. 22-31) — the sufferer declares God's praise 'in the midst of the assembly' (Heb 2:12) and 'all the ends of the earth shall remember and turn to the LORD.'

jewish_view.explanation: Judaism reads Psalm 22 as David's personal lament during a time of persecution (perhaps by Saul or during Absalom's revolt). The psalm follows the standard pattern of an individual lament: complaint (vv.1-21) followed by thanksgiving (vv.22-31). The descriptions are poetic and metaphorical, not literal predictive detail. 'Dogs surround me' (v.16) = enemies, not Roman soldiers. The critical phrase 'they pierced my hands and feet' (v.16) is contested textually — the Masoretic Hebrew reads 'ka'ari' (like a lion [they maul] my hands and feet), not 'karu' (they pierced). The psalm is not in the Talmud's messianic proof-text lists. It is David's experience, universalized for all righteous sufferers.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The key textual crux is verse 16b. The MT reads 'ka'ari yadai v'raglai' = 'like a lion, my hands and my feet' — which is grammatically incomplete (the verb is missing). The LXX reads 'oryxan cheiras mou kai podas' = 'they dug/pierced my hands and my feet.' A Nahal Hever scroll (5/6HevPs, ~50-68 AD) reads 'ka'aru' (they pierced) — a Hebrew reading that matches the LXX, not the MT. This is one of the most significant textual variants in the entire Hebrew Bible. The MT reading requires a verb to be supplied ('like a lion [they maul/bite] my hands and feet'); the 5/6HevPs / LXX reading is grammatically complete. Crucifixion was a Persian invention (~6th c. BC), adopted by Rome — Psalm 22 predates it by centuries if Davidic authorship (~1000 BC) is accepted.

prophecies.title: The Divine King — Your Throne, O God, Is Forever
prophecies.text_snippet: Your throne, O God, is forever and ever; the scepter of your kingdom is a scepter of uprightness. You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness; therefore God, your God, has anointed you

christian_view.explanation: Hebrews 1:8-9 quotes Psalm 45:6-7 directly and applies it to Christ, making one of the clearest NT claims of Christ's deity from the OT. The argument: God the Father addresses the Son as 'God' — 'Your throne, O God, is forever.' Then the text distinguishes the Son from the Father: 'therefore God, YOUR God, has anointed you' — the Son is called God, yet has a God who anoints Him. This paradox of a divine figure who is both 'God' and has a 'God' points to the Trinitarian distinction of persons within the Godhead.

jewish_view.explanation: Jewish commentators offer alternative translations to avoid the implication that a human king is called 'God.' Rashi and Ibn Ezra read it as 'Your throne is the throne of God' or 'Your divine throne' — the word 'elohim' is used as an adjective meaning 'divine/mighty,' not as a vocative address. Alternatively, 'elohim' can refer to judges or mighty ones (cf. Exodus 21:6; Psalm 82:6), so the verse means 'your throne, O judge/mighty one.' The psalm is a wedding song (shirah) for a king — probably Solomon — marrying a foreign princess. It is royal court poetry, not a prophecy of a divine Messiah.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The grammar of 'kisse'akha elohim olam va'ed' is genuinely ambiguous. Options: (1) 'Your throne, O God, is forever' — elohim as vocative (address); (2) 'Your throne is God's throne forever' — elohim as genitive; (3) 'Your throne is like God's, forever' — comparative. Option (1) is the most natural reading of the Hebrew syntax and is how the LXX understood it ('ho thronos sou ho theos'). The psalm's Sitz im Leben is a royal wedding (note the queen, ivory palaces, myrrh), but royal psalms were reread messianically once the monarchy ended in 586 BC. The 'anointing' (mashach) in v.7 connects to Messiah (mashiach). Psalm 45 is superscribed 'A Song of Loves' (shir yedidot) — unique in the Psalter.

prophecies.title: Zeal for God's House — The Suffering Servant's Reproach
prophecies.text_snippet: Zeal for Your house has consumed me, and the reproaches of those who reproach You have fallen on me... They gave me gall for my food, and for my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink

christian_view.explanation: Psalm 69 is the second most-quoted psalm in the NT (after Psalm 22). John 2:17 applies 'zeal for your house has consumed me' to Jesus cleansing the temple. John 19:28-29 connects the vinegar (v.21) to the crucifixion. Romans 15:3 applies v.9b to Christ bearing reproach. Acts 1:20 applies the imprecation (v.25) to Judas's replacement. The psalm's structure — innocent suffering followed by vindication — maps onto Christ's passion and exaltation.

jewish_view.explanation: The psalm is read as David's personal lament during persecution. The descriptions are metaphorical — David is 'drowning' in trouble, surrounded by enemies who mock his piety. The imprecatory sections (vv.22-28) are prayers for God to judge David's persecutors — standard in lament psalms. These curses are problematic if applied to Jesus, who taught 'love your enemies.' Christianity must selectively apply parts of the psalm to Christ while ignoring others (e.g., v.5: 'O God, You know my folly, and my wrongs are not hidden from You' — Christ, being sinless, could not say this). The psalm is David's, not the Messiah's.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The tension in Christian application is real: verse 5 has the psalmist confessing sin ('my folly... my wrongs'), which is incompatible with applying the psalm to a sinless Messiah. Christian interpreters handle this by distinguishing between what is typological (the suffering pattern) and what is personal to David (the confession of sin). The NT never quotes v.5. This selective quotation method was standard in Second Temple hermeneutics (pesher interpretation at Qumran works similarly). The 'gall and vinegar' of v.21 is often cited as predictive of crucifixion, though both substances were common in the ancient world.

prophecies.title: The Worldwide Reign of the Righteous King
prophecies.text_snippet: He shall have dominion from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth... All kings shall fall down before Him; all nations shall serve Him

christian_view.explanation: While Christ has been given 'all authority in heaven and on earth' (Matt 28:18), the visible worldwide reign described in Psalm 72 — universal justice, the end of poverty, all nations serving Him — awaits the Second Coming and the consummation of the Kingdom. The psalm describes a just ruler who delivers the poor (v.12-14), whose reign produces abundance (v.16), and whose name endures forever (v.17). These descriptions exceed anything Solomon achieved and point to the Messiah's future kingdom (Rev 11:15). The 'already/not yet' framework applies: Christ reigns now spiritually, fully in the age to come.

jewish_view.explanation: Psalm 72 is understood as a prayer for Solomon (superscription: 'of Solomon') that doubles as a vision of the ideal messianic king. The descriptions — universal dominion, justice for the poor, lasting peace, all nations bowing — far exceed Solomon's historical reign and point to the future Messiah. This is one of the clearest descriptions of the messianic age in the Psalter: a world at peace under a righteous Davidic king. Since this has clearly not happened, the Messiah has not yet come. The psalm's concluding doxology (vv.18-19) ends Book II of the Psalter, making it a climactic statement of messianic hope.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Psalm 72 is notable because both Jewish and Christian traditions agree it is messianic and both agree it is largely unfulfilled. The difference is that Christians identify the Messiah as Jesus (reigning now, fully later), while Judaism awaits a future human Davidic king. The superscription 'of Solomon' (lishlomoh) could mean 'by Solomon,' 'for Solomon,' or 'about/concerning Solomon.' The description of universal dominion ('from sea to sea, from the River to the ends of the earth,' v.8) echoes the Abrahamic land promise extended to cosmic scope. The Hebrew 'yered' (v.6, 'he shall come down like rain') uses imagery of divine blessing that exceeds normal royal language.

prophecies.title: The Priest-King — Sit at My Right Hand
prophecies.text_snippet: The LORD says to my Lord: 'Sit at My right hand until I make Your enemies a footstool for Your feet'... You are a priest forever according to the order of Melchizedek

christian_view.explanation: Psalm 110 is the most frequently quoted OT passage in the NT. Jesus Himself used it to confound the Pharisees (Matt 22:41-46): if the Messiah is David's son, why does David call him 'my Lord' (adoni)? Peter quotes it at Pentecost (Acts 2:34-35) to prove Christ's ascension and heavenly session. Hebrews builds its entire Christological argument on v.4: Christ is a priest 'according to the order of Melchizedek' — a priesthood that supersedes the Levitical order because it is eternal and not dependent on genealogy (Heb 7). Christ now sits at God's right hand (Eph 1:20), reigning until all enemies are subdued (1 Cor 15:25).

jewish_view.explanation: Rashi reads Psalm 110 as David speaking about Abraham (not about the Messiah): 'The LORD said to my master Abraham, sit at my right hand.' This avoids the problem of David calling a future descendant 'my lord.' Alternatively, some rabbis read 'adoni' as referring to David himself, with the psalm composed by a court poet. The Melchizedek priesthood (v.4) is not seen as superseding Levitical priesthood; Melchizedek's role ended when the Aaronic priesthood was established at Sinai. The Talmud does discuss this psalm in messianic contexts (Sanhedrin 108b; Nedarim 32b), but the dominant medieval reading (Rashi, Radak) applies it to Abraham, avoiding the Christological implications.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The critical word is 'adoni' (my lord) vs. 'Adonai' (the LORD). The MT vowel pointing distinguishes them: 'YHWH said to adoni' = 'the LORD said to my lord.' If David wrote the psalm, he calls someone 'my lord' — a superior. In Israelite kingship theology, the only one who outranks the king is God. Jesus's argument (Matt 22:43-45) depends on Davidic authorship: if David calls the Messiah 'lord,' the Messiah must be more than David's descendant. The combination of king (v.1-3) and priest (v.4) in one figure is remarkable — these were separate offices in Israel. Uzziah was struck with leprosy for attempting to combine them (2 Chr 26:16-21). The psalm implies an eschatological figure who legitimately holds both offices. Zechariah 6:12-13 similarly envisions a priest 'on his throne.'

prophecies.title: The Rejected Stone Becomes the Cornerstone
prophecies.text_snippet: The stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone. This is the LORD's doing; it is marvelous in our eyes

christian_view.fulfillment_ref: Matthew 21:42; Mark 12:10-11; Luke 20:17; Acts 4:11; 1 Peter 2:7; Ephesians 2:20
christian_view.explanation: Jesus applied this directly to Himself in the Parable of the Wicked Tenants (Matt 21:42), telling the religious leaders that the stone THEY rejected would become the cornerstone — they would stumble over it and be crushed. Peter used it before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4:11) as proof that the crucified and risen Jesus is the foundation of God's building. Paul calls Christ the 'cornerstone' in whom the whole structure of the church is joined together (Eph 2:20). The pattern of rejection-then-exaltation is the gospel in miniature.

jewish_view.explanation: Traditional Jewish interpretation applies this to Israel itself — the nation rejected and despised by the empires (Egypt, Babylon, Persia, Greece, Rome) yet chosen by God as the cornerstone of His purposes. The Midrash (Shemot Rabbah 29:9) tells of a stone rejected by all the builders but found to be the foundation stone — applied to Israel or to David, who was initially rejected by his brothers (1 Sam 16) and by Saul's court. The psalm is liturgically central: it is sung during Hallel (Passover, Sukkot, Shavuot) and the 'hosanna' (hoshia na, v.25) is waved with the lulav on Sukkot. Some rabbinic sources also apply it to the Messiah — rejected then vindicated.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Psalm 118 is a processional thanksgiving liturgy for the Temple. The 'stone' metaphor likely originated in building practice — a stone rejected during construction turns out to be the crucial piece. The term 'rosh pinnah' (head of the corner) could mean 'cornerstone' (foundation) or 'capstone' (top of an arch or wall). The distinction matters theologically: is the figure the foundation (what everything is built on) or the crowning completion? The NT uses both images (Eph 2:20 = cornerstone; 1 Pet 2:7 = head of corner). Isaiah 28:16 adds another stone image ('a tested stone, a costly cornerstone for the foundation') that the NT also applies to Christ. The triple stone testimony (Ps 118, Isa 8:14, Isa 28:16) appears combined in 1 Peter 2:6-8.

prophecies.title: The Virgin Birth — Immanuel
prophecies.text_snippet: Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel

christian_view.explanation: Matthew 1:22-23 explicitly quotes Isaiah 7:14 as fulfilled in Jesus's virgin birth: 'All this took place to fulfill what was spoken by the Lord through the prophet.' The name 'Immanuel' (God with us) is understood as a declaration of Christ's divine nature — He IS God with us, not merely named symbolically. The LXX translation 'parthenos' (virgin) confirms the pre-Christian Jewish understanding of the term as referring to a virgin. The sign is miraculous precisely because a virgin conceiving is beyond nature.

jewish_view.explanation: This is perhaps the single most debated verse between Judaism and Christianity. The Hebrew word is 'almah,' which means 'young woman of marriageable age,' not necessarily 'virgin' (which would be 'betulah'). Rashi and Ibn Ezra identify the 'almah' as Isaiah's wife or a young woman in Ahaz's court. The prophecy had an immediate fulfillment: a child born in Ahaz's time whose early childhood would mark the timeframe for Assyria's defeat of Syria and Ephraim (7:15-16). The 'sign' is not a miraculous birth but a timing prediction. 'Immanuel' is a symbolic name (like Isaiah's other children — Maher-shalal-hash-baz, Shear-jashub) meaning 'God is with us' in the sense of divine protection, not incarnation.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The 'almah/betulah/parthenos' debate is complex. 'Almah' occurs 7 times in the Hebrew Bible (Gen 24:43; Ex 2:8; Ps 68:25; Prov 30:19; Song 1:3; 6:8; Isa 7:14). In every case, the woman appears to be a virgin, but the word's semantic range is 'young woman' — virginity is implied by youth, not explicitly denoted. 'Betulah' usually means 'virgin' but can sometimes mean simply 'young woman' (Joel 1:8 — a betulah mourning 'the husband of her youth'). The LXX translators (~250 BC) chose 'parthenos' (unambiguously 'virgin'), suggesting they understood a miraculous element. The dual-fulfillment view holds that the prophecy had a near fulfillment in Isaiah's day (a young woman bearing a son) and an ultimate fulfillment in Christ (a virgin bearing a son). The definite article 'ha-almah' (THE young woman) suggests a specific, known figure — debated whether this makes it more or less likely to be messianic.

prophecies.title: Wonderful Counselor — The Divine Child on David's Throne
prophecies.text_snippet: For a child will be born to us, a son will be given to us; and the government will rest on His shoulders; and His name will be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Eternal Father, Prince of Peace

christian_view.explanation: Christians read these throne names as divine titles belonging to Christ. 'Mighty God' (El Gibbor) is a title used of YHWH Himself in Isaiah 10:21, establishing the child's deity. 'Eternal Father' (Avi-Ad) means 'Father of Eternity' — the one who authors/possesses eternity. 'Prince of Peace' (Sar Shalom) describes the nature of His kingdom. The 'child born / son given' is the incarnation — a child born (human nature) and a son given (pre-existent divine Son sent from the Father). The 'throne of David' is fulfilled in Christ's kingship, with the eternal, universal peace-kingdom yet to be fully manifested.

jewish_view.explanation: The traditional Jewish reading varies. Some (Rashi) apply this to Hezekiah, Ahaz's son, whose birth is the 'sign' that God will deliver Judah from Assyria. The titles would then be throne names (like Egyptian pharaonic titulary) given at coronation, not declarations of deity. 'El Gibbor' in this context means 'a mighty hero' or 'God is mighty' — a theophoric name, not a claim that the child IS God. Other Jewish interpreters (Radak, Abarbanel) acknowledge a messianic reading but see the Messiah as a future human king, not a divine being. The titles describe God's relationship to the king ('God has counseled wonderfully for him; the Mighty God is his eternal father; he is a prince of peace'), not the king's own nature.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The four throne names follow the pattern of Egyptian royal titulary, where each pharaoh received a set of throne names at coronation. This ANE background supports a coronation psalm reading. However, 'El Gibbor' is uniquely strong — in Isaiah 10:21, it refers to YHWH Himself. If applied to a human king, this is unprecedented in the Hebrew Bible. The Targum Jonathan translates this passage messianically but rewrites it to avoid attributing deity to the Messiah: 'His name has been called from of old: Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, He who lives forever, the Messiah, in whose days peace shall increase upon us.' Note how the Targum separates the names — making 'Mighty God' refer to God who does the naming, not the child being named. The Hebrew syntax, however, most naturally reads all four titles as belonging to the child.

prophecies.title: The Root of Jesse — Spirit-Anointed Ruler
prophecies.text_snippet: A shoot will spring from the stem of Jesse, and a Branch from his roots will bear fruit. The Spirit of the LORD will rest on Him... He will strike the earth with the rod of His mouth

christian_view.explanation: Jesus is 'the Root of Jesse' (Rev 22:16) and the 'Branch' (netzer, possibly echoed in 'Nazarene' — Matt 2:23). The sevenfold Spirit (11:2) rested on Him at baptism (Matt 3:16). He judges with righteousness and strikes the earth with the rod of His mouth (cf. Rev 19:15). The 'peaceable kingdom' (wolf with lamb, v.6-9) awaits the millennial/eternal state. The 'banner for the peoples' (v.10) is the cross, to which Gentile nations rally. Romans 15:12 quotes v.10 to support the Gentile mission: 'the root of Jesse... in Him shall the Gentiles hope.'

jewish_view.explanation: Isaiah 11 is one of the most important messianic texts in Judaism and is almost universally read as messianic in rabbinic tradition. The Messiah will be a descendant of David (from Jesse's line), anointed with God's Spirit, who will judge justly, defeat the wicked, and usher in an age of universal peace (the wolf lying with the lamb). This has manifestly not happened — there is no world peace, no end to violence, no universal knowledge of God (v.9). Therefore, the Messiah has not come. Maimonides (Hilkhot Melakhim 11:1) uses this chapter as the standard for identifying the Messiah: he must be a Davidic king who fights Israel's wars, rebuilds the Temple, and gathers the exiles. If he succeeds in all these, he is certainly the Messiah.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The word 'netzer' (branch/shoot, v.1) is rare, appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible with this meaning. Matthew 2:23 ('He shall be called a Nazarene') may be a wordplay on netzer, though the connection is debated. The peaceable kingdom imagery (vv.6-9) is either literal (a transformation of nature in the messianic age — the Jewish view) or metaphorical (the reconciliation of hostile peoples — some Christian interpretations) or both. The phrase 'the earth will be full of the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea' (v.9) is a universal scope claim that both traditions agree is unfulfilled. The 'root' and 'branch' imagery connects to Jeremiah 23:5 (the Righteous Branch) and Zechariah 3:8; 6:12 (the Branch). The Qumran community used 'Branch of David' as a messianic title (4QFlorilegium; 4QpIsa).

prophecies.title: First Servant Song — The Gentle Justice-Bringer
prophecies.text_snippet: Behold My Servant, whom I uphold, My chosen one in whom My soul delights. I have put My Spirit upon Him; He will bring forth justice to the nations

christian_view.explanation: Matthew 12:18-21 quotes Isaiah 42:1-4 at length and applies it to Jesus's gentle, non-confrontational ministry style — 'He will not quarrel or cry out... a bruised reed He will not break.' The Father's words at Jesus's baptism ('This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased,' Matt 3:17) echo 'My chosen one in whom My soul delights.' The servant's mission to 'bring forth justice to the nations' and be 'a light to the nations' (42:6) is fulfilled in the Gentile mission of the church.

jewish_view.explanation: The dominant Jewish interpretation identifies the 'Servant' in the Servant Songs as the nation of Israel collectively. Isaiah himself says 'You are My servant, Israel' (41:8; 44:1; 49:3). Israel is God's chosen servant-nation, called to be 'a light to the nations' (42:6) by preserving monotheism and Torah. The servant's suffering (especially in Isaiah 53) is Israel's suffering among hostile nations throughout history. Rashi, Ibn Ezra, and Radak all hold the collective Israel reading. However, some earlier sources (Targum Jonathan, some Talmudic passages) do read individual Servant Songs messianically — there was not one monolithic view in ancient Judaism.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The four Servant Songs (42:1-9; 49:1-13; 50:4-11; 52:13-53:12) were first identified as a distinct group by Bernhard Duhm in 1892. Whether they describe the same figure is debated. The tension in the text is real: sometimes the servant IS Israel (41:8; 44:1), sometimes the servant has a mission TO Israel (49:5-6). This has led to proposals of: (1) corporate Israel; (2) ideal/faithful Israel (a remnant within the nation); (3) an individual prophet (Isaiah himself, or a future figure); (4) a royal/messianic figure. The fluidity between individual and corporate identity is characteristic of Hebrew 'corporate personality' thinking — the king embodies the nation, and the nation is embodied in the king.

prophecies.title: Fourth Servant Song — The Suffering Servant
prophecies.text_snippet: He was pierced for our transgressions, He was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on Him, and by His wounds we are healed... the LORD has laid on Him the iniquity of us all

christian_view.fulfillment_ref: Acts 8:32-35; Romans 4:25; 1 Peter 2:22-25; Mark 15:28; Luke 22:37; 1 Corinthians 15:3; Philippians 2:7-8
christian_view.explanation: Isaiah 53 is the most detailed prophetic portrait of Christ's atoning death in the OT. The chapter describes: vicarious/substitutionary suffering ('pierced for OUR transgressions,' v.5), voluntary submission ('like a lamb led to slaughter,' v.7), innocence ('He had done no violence, nor was any deceit in His mouth,' v.9), death with the wicked and burial with the rich (v.9 — crucified between criminals, buried in Joseph of Arimathea's tomb), and ultimate vindication ('He will see His offspring, He will prolong His days,' v.10 — resurrection). The Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8:32-35) was reading this passage when Philip explained the gospel. Peter quotes it directly (1 Pet 2:24). This is the theological foundation of penal substitutionary atonement.

jewish_view.explanation: The dominant rabbinic interpretation since Rashi (11th c.) identifies the Suffering Servant as the nation of Israel, suffering unjustly among the nations. 'He was despised and rejected' describes Jewish persecution. 'He bore our griefs' means the nations THOUGHT Israel was being punished by God, but in fact Israel was suffering innocently. The 'we' who speak in 53:1-6 are the Gentile nations who finally recognize Israel's innocence. However, pre-Rashi Jewish sources tell a different story: Targum Jonathan (1st-2nd c. AD) applies Isaiah 53 to the Messiah, though it transfers the suffering away from the Messiah onto Israel's enemies. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 98b) discusses a suffering Messiah ('Messiah the Leper') who sits at the gates of Rome, bandaging his wounds. The Zohar applies 53:5 to the Messiah. The collective Israel reading became dominant partly in response to Christian polemics.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Isaiah 53 is the most theologically contested chapter in the Hebrew Bible. Key observations: (1) The 'servant' in ch.53 is distinguished FROM Israel — in 53:8, 'he was stricken for the transgression of MY PEOPLE,' where 'my people' = Israel, and the servant suffers FOR them. If the servant IS Israel, who are 'my people'? (2) The servant is an innocent individual (no violence, no deceit, v.9) — difficult to apply to any nation. (3) The servant dies and is buried (v.9) but then 'sees his offspring' and 'prolongs his days' (v.10) — implying resurrection. (4) The suffering is explicitly vicarious and substitutionary (vv.4-6, 10-12). The pre-Christian Jewish reading was more diverse than the later unified 'Israel' reading. The shift to the collective interpretation accelerated after Jewish-Christian polemic in the medieval period. Daniel Boyarin and other scholars have documented the messianic reading's antiquity. The Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsa-a) from Qumran preserves a text substantially identical to the MT.

prophecies.title: Second Servant Song — A Light to the Nations
prophecies.text_snippet: I will also make You a light of the nations, so that My salvation may reach to the end of the earth

christian_view.explanation: Simeon quotes Isaiah 49:6 in his song at the Temple, calling the infant Jesus 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles' (Luke 2:32). Paul and Barnabas cite it in Acts 13:47 to justify their Gentile mission: 'I have placed You as a light for the Gentiles, that You may bring salvation to the end of the earth.' The Servant in 49:5-6 has a mission TO Israel ('to bring Jacob back to Him') as well as to the Gentiles — Christ came first to 'the lost sheep of the house of Israel' (Matt 15:24) and then commissioned the worldwide mission (Matt 28:19).

jewish_view.explanation: The servant here is identified as Israel (49:3, 'You are My Servant, Israel'), though v.5 says the servant has a mission to 'bring Jacob back' — creating the tension of the servant being Israel yet ministering to Israel. Some resolve this by distinguishing ideal Israel (the faithful remnant / the prophet) from empirical Israel (the nation as a whole). Israel's calling is to be 'a light to the nations' — bringing ethical monotheism to the world. This is partially fulfilled through Judaism's historical influence on Christianity and Islam (both monotheistic derivatives), but fully fulfilled only in the messianic age when 'the earth will be full of the knowledge of the LORD' (Isa 11:9).

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Isaiah 49:3 explicitly names the servant as 'Israel,' but v.5 gives the servant a mission to restore Israel — creating an apparent contradiction. Solutions: (1) 'Israel' in v.3 is a gloss (some LXX manuscripts omit it); (2) the servant is the ideal embodiment of Israel who works to restore the actual nation; (3) the servant is an individual from within Israel (a prophet or future figure) who represents and restores the whole. The concept of 'corporate personality' in Hebrew thought allows an individual to embody a group and vice versa. The 'light to the nations' theme (42:6; 49:6) became central to Second Temple Judaism's self-understanding (cf. Wisdom of Solomon 18:4; Tobit 13:11).

prophecies.title: New Heavens and New Earth
prophecies.text_snippet: For behold, I create new heavens and a new earth; and the former things will not be remembered or come to mind

christian_view.explanation: Peter directly quotes Isaiah 65:17 in 2 Peter 3:13 ('we are looking for new heavens and a new earth, in which righteousness dwells'). Revelation 21:1-4 describes the vision's fulfillment: 'I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth passed away.' The description in Isaiah 65:17-25 includes elements of both a renewed earthly order (longevity, building houses, planting vineyards) and a transformed creation (no more weeping, the wolf and lamb together). Christians debate whether this is the eternal state or a millennial kingdom preceding it.

jewish_view.explanation: The new heavens and new earth vision is central to Jewish eschatology. It describes the messianic age or the 'world to come' (olam ha-ba). Maimonides understood this more figuratively — the 'new world' is a renewed social and spiritual order under the Messiah, not necessarily a physical cosmic re-creation. Other rabbis (Nachmanides) took it more literally. The details in 65:20 (people dying at 100 being considered young) suggest a transformed but still mortal earthly existence — this aligns better with the messianic age than with a fully transcendent afterlife. The vision is entirely unfulfilled and awaits the Messiah's coming.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Isaiah 65-66 is attributed by most critical scholars to 'Third Isaiah' (Trito-Isaiah), writing in the post-exilic period (late 6th-5th c. BC). The new creation language represents the most expansive eschatological vision in the Hebrew prophets. The tension between continuity and discontinuity with the present order is significant: Isaiah 65:20 describes lifespans of hundreds of years (continuity with mortality) while 65:17 says the 'former things will not be remembered' (radical discontinuity). This dual character gave rise to the rabbinic distinction between 'the days of the Messiah' (an earthly messianic kingdom) and 'the world to come' (a fully transcendent state). Christian millennialism (Rev 20) may reflect this same two-stage structure.

prophecies.title: The Son of Man — Everlasting Dominion
prophecies.text_snippet: One like a Son of Man was coming with the clouds of heaven... to Him was given dominion, glory, and a kingdom, that all the peoples, nations, and languages should serve Him

christian_view.explanation: 'Son of Man' was Jesus's preferred self-designation (~80 times in the Gospels), and it comes directly from Daniel 7:13. At His trial before the Sanhedrin, Jesus quoted Daniel 7:13 combined with Psalm 110:1: 'You will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds of heaven' (Mark 14:62) — the high priest tore his robes, recognizing the claim. The figure in Daniel 7 approaches the 'Ancient of Days' (God) and receives universal, everlasting dominion — this is not a mere human king. Christ ascended into the clouds (Acts 1:9) and will return on the clouds (Rev 1:7). The kingdom was inaugurated at the ascension; the visible, universal dominion awaits the Second Coming.

jewish_view.explanation: The interpretation of the 'Son of Man' figure in Daniel 7 varies in Jewish tradition. (1) Rashi and Ibn Ezra read 'one like a son of man' as a collective symbol for Israel / the saints of the Most High (7:18, 27), contrasted with the beast-empires. Just as the four beasts represent kingdoms, the human figure represents Israel's kingdom. (2) Other rabbinic sources (Sanhedrin 98a; 1 Enoch's 'Parables,' which influenced Jewish thought) read the figure as an individual messianic king. Rabbi Akiva initially interpreted the plural 'thrones' (7:9) as one for God and one for David/Messiah (Sanhedrin 38b), though he later retracted under pressure from Rabbi Yose. Either way, the Messiah has not yet received universal dominion.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The phrase 'bar enash' (Aramaic for 'son of man') literally means 'a human being' or 'one like a human.' The contrast in Daniel 7 is between beast-like empires (rising from the sea = chaos) and a human-like figure (coming with clouds = heaven). The 'clouds of heaven' are significant: in the Hebrew Bible, cloud-riding is exclusively a divine attribute (Ps 104:3; Isa 19:1; Deut 33:26). For a human-like figure to ride the clouds suggests a divine or semi-divine status. The Similitudes of Enoch (1 Enoch 37-71, possibly 1st c. BC) develop the Son of Man into a pre-existent, enthroned, messianic judge — showing that pre-Christian Judaism had already expanded Daniel's vision beyond a mere symbol for Israel. The 4 Ezra 'Man from the Sea' (ch.13) is another Second Temple development of this figure.

prophecies.title: The Seventy Weeks — Messiah Cut Off
prophecies.text_snippet: Seventy weeks are decreed for your people... to anoint the Most Holy Place. From the issuing of a decree to restore and rebuild Jerusalem until Messiah the Prince, there will be seven weeks and sixty-two weeks... Then after the sixty-two weeks the Messiah will be cut off and have nothing

christian_view.fulfillment_date: ~30 AD (Messiah cut off); future (70th week debated)
christian_view.explanation: Christians read the 'seventy weeks' as 70 x 7 = 490 years. Starting from Artaxerxes' decree to rebuild Jerusalem (445 BC, Neh 2:1-8), 69 weeks (483 years) brings us to approximately 30-33 AD — the time of Christ's public ministry and death. The prophecy states the Messiah will be 'cut off' (killed) and 'have nothing' (or 'not for himself' — His death is vicarious). After this, 'the people of the prince who is to come will destroy the city and the sanctuary' — fulfilled in 70 AD by the Romans. The 70th week is debated: preterists see it as the 3.5 years of ministry plus 3.5 years after the cross; dispensationalists see a 'gap' with the 70th week being a future tribulation period.

jewish_view.explanation: Jewish interpreters calculate differently and identify different figures. Rashi identifies the 'anointed one' (mashiach) of 9:25 as Cyrus the Great (called 'His anointed' in Isa 45:1), and the 'anointed one cut off' (9:26) as the high priest Onias III, murdered in 171 BC during the Seleucid crisis. The 'prince who is to come' is Titus (70 AD) or Antiochus IV. The seventy weeks span the period of the Second Temple (~516 BC to 70 AD). The word 'mashiach' here means 'anointed one' (priest or king), not necessarily THE Messiah. Saadia Gaon and others offer different chronological calculations. The Talmud (Megillah 12a; Nazir 32b) discusses the 70 weeks but does not use them as a messianic proof text for a specific future figure.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The chronology of Daniel 9 is notoriously difficult. The 'weeks' (shavu'im) are literally 'sevens' — almost certainly seven-year periods, yielding 490 years. But the starting point is debated: (1) Cyrus's decree, 538 BC; (2) Artaxerxes to Ezra, 458 BC; (3) Artaxerxes to Nehemiah, 445 BC. Each yields a different endpoint. The traditional Christian calculation (Sir Robert Anderson, 1895) uses 445 BC + 483 prophetic years (360 days each) = AD 32, Palm Sunday — impressively precise but dependent on assumptions about calendar systems. Critical scholars often date Daniel to the Maccabean period (~165 BC) and see the 490 years as an approximation from Jeremiah's 70 years (Jer 25:11-12) x 7, spanning from 586 BC to the Maccabean crisis. The term 'mashiach nagid' (anointed prince, v.25) is unique to Daniel and appears to be a royal title, not just a generic 'anointed one.' The phrase 'cut off' (yikkaret) echoes the covenant curse of being 'cut off from the people' — execution or violent death.

prophecies.title: Resurrection of the Dead
prophecies.text_snippet: Many of those who sleep in the dust of the ground will awake, these to everlasting life, but the others to disgrace and everlasting contempt. Those who have insight will shine brightly like the expanse of heaven

christian_view.explanation: Daniel 12:2 is the clearest OT statement of bodily resurrection with a dual outcome (life vs. contempt). Jesus echoes it directly in John 5:28-29: 'an hour is coming in which all who are in the tombs will hear His voice, and will come forth — those who did the good deeds to a resurrection of life, those who committed evil to a resurrection of judgment.' Paul builds his resurrection theology on this foundation (1 Cor 15). The 'shining like stars' (12:3) is applied to the glorification of believers (Matt 13:43; Phil 2:15). This awaits fulfillment at the general resurrection.

jewish_view.explanation: Bodily resurrection (techiyat ha-metim) is a core tenet of rabbinic Judaism — Maimonides lists it as the 13th principle of faith. Daniel 12:2 is the primary biblical proof text. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 90a-92b) devotes an extensive discussion to proving resurrection from the Torah, with Daniel 12 as the clearest reference. The Amidah prayer (recited three times daily) praises God as 'mechayeh ha-metim' (who resurrects the dead). However, resurrection occurs in the messianic age or the world to come — it has not happened yet. The 'wise who shine like stars' refers to Torah scholars and the righteous. Sadducees rejected resurrection; Pharisaic/rabbinic Judaism affirmed it.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Daniel 12:2 is the latest and most explicit resurrection text in the Hebrew Bible. Earlier hints include Isaiah 26:19 ('Your dead will live; their corpses will rise') and Ezekiel 37 (valley of dry bones — though this may be metaphorical for national restoration). The word 'many' (rabbim) in 12:2 is debated: does it mean 'many' (not all) or 'the many' (= the multitude, i.e., all)? If 'many but not all,' the text may envision resurrection for the notably righteous and notably wicked, with the ordinary dead remaining in Sheol. The astral imagery ('shine like stars') parallels 1 Enoch 104:2 and suggests a transformed, glorified existence. Daniel 12:1 mentions Michael the archangel 'standing up' for Israel — connecting resurrection to divine council warfare and eschatological deliverance.

prophecies.title: The New Covenant
prophecies.text_snippet: Behold, days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah, not like the covenant which I made with their fathers... I will put My law within them and on their heart I will write it

christian_view.fulfillment_date: ~30 AD (inaugurated at the Last Supper / Calvary)
christian_view.explanation: Jesus explicitly invoked this prophecy at the Last Supper: 'This cup is the new covenant in My blood' (Luke 22:20; 1 Cor 11:25). Hebrews 8:6-13 quotes Jeremiah 31:31-34 in full — the longest OT quotation in the NT — and argues that the new covenant in Christ has made the old covenant 'obsolete' (8:13). The new covenant's characteristics are: (1) internal — law written on hearts, not tablets (2 Cor 3:3); (2) universal knowledge of God — no need for external teaching (1 John 2:27); (3) complete forgiveness — 'I will remember their sins no more' (Heb 10:17). The Holy Spirit is the agent who writes the law on hearts (2 Cor 3:6).

jewish_view.explanation: Judaism interprets the 'new covenant' not as a replacement of the Torah but as a renewed, deeper internalization of the SAME Torah. The content of the covenant does not change — the text says 'I will put MY LAW within them' (v.33), meaning the Torah itself. What changes is the mode: instead of external tablets, the Torah will be inscribed on human hearts so that obedience becomes natural. This awaits the messianic age. Critically, vv.35-37 promise that Israel will exist as a nation 'forever' — the new covenant does not abolish or replace Israel's covenant relationship with God. The idea that God replaced Israel with the Church ('supersessionism') contradicts the very passage Christians cite as its basis.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The phrase 'new covenant' (berit chadashah) occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. The Qumran community called themselves 'those who entered the new covenant in the land of Damascus' (CD 6:19; 8:21), showing the concept was already operative in pre-Christian Judaism. The key exegetical question is whether 'new' means 'replacement' (as Hebrews argues) or 'renewal' (as Judaism reads it). The Hebrew 'chadash' can mean either 'brand new' or 'renewed' (cf. 'chadesh yameinu k'kedem' = 'renew our days as of old,' Lam 5:21). Ezekiel 36:26-27 parallels this passage with 'a new heart and a new spirit' — both envision internal transformation rather than external law. The enduring debate: is the new covenant a different covenant with different terms, or the same covenant experienced differently?

prophecies.title: The Righteous Branch — The LORD Our Righteousness
prophecies.text_snippet: I will raise up for David a righteous Branch; and He will reign as king and act wisely and do justice and righteousness in the land... His name will be called: The LORD our Righteousness

christian_view.explanation: Christ is the 'Righteous Branch' from David's line. Paul declares that Christ 'became for us... righteousness' (1 Cor 1:30) and that God 'made Him who knew no sin to be sin on our behalf, so that we might become the righteousness of God in Him' (2 Cor 5:21). The name 'YHWH Tsidkenu' (The LORD Our Righteousness) applies to Jesus as the one through whom God's righteousness is imputed to believers. The visible reign of justice and righteousness (23:5) awaits the Second Coming, when Christ returns as 'Faithful and True' and judges in righteousness (Rev 19:11).

jewish_view.explanation: This is a clearly messianic text in rabbinic tradition. The Talmud (Bava Batra 75b) lists 'YHWH Tsidkenu' as one of the names of the Messiah. The 'Branch' (tsemach) became a standard messianic title in Second Temple Judaism (cf. Zech 3:8; 6:12). But the Messiah is a righteous HUMAN king from David's line who will reign in justice — 'the LORD our Righteousness' is a theophoric name meaning 'the LORD is our righteousness' (God vindicates through this king), not a claim that the king IS YHWH. In Jeremiah 33:16, the same name is applied to Jerusalem, showing it is a title of status, not an identification with God. The Messiah has not come because justice and righteousness do not yet characterize the land.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The name 'YHWH Tsidkenu' (23:6) is a deliberate wordplay on King Zedekiah (Tsidkiyyahu = 'YHWH is my righteousness'), the last king of Judah. Jeremiah, who lived through Zedekiah's catastrophic reign, promises a future Davidic king who will live up to the name that Zedekiah disgraced. The 'Branch' (tsemach) terminology connects Jeremiah's promise to Isaiah 11:1 (netzer/branch) and Zechariah 3:8; 6:12 (tsemach). The Qumran texts refer to the 'Branch of David' (tsemach David) as a messianic title (4QFlor 1:11; 4QpIsa). Interestingly, Jer 33:15-16 repeats the promise but applies the name 'YHWH Tsidkenu' to Jerusalem rather than to the king — this complicates the Christian reading that the name implies the king's deity.

prophecies.title: Valley of Dry Bones — National Resurrection
prophecies.text_snippet: Son of man, can these bones live?... I will put My Spirit in you and you will live, and I will place you on your own land

christian_view.explanation: Christians see multiple layers: (1) Spiritual resurrection — believers are 'made alive' from spiritual death (Eph 2:1-5), the Spirit giving new life. (2) National restoration of Israel — Paul argues in Romans 11 that Israel's hardening is temporary and 'all Israel will be saved' (11:26), which would be 'life from the dead' (11:15). (3) Physical resurrection — the imagery of dead bones coming back to life points toward the general resurrection (Rev 20). Some see the modern State of Israel as a partial fulfillment ('bones coming together,' v.7-8) awaiting the spiritual dimension ('breath/spirit,' v.9-10).

jewish_view.explanation: Ezekiel 37 is primarily understood as a prophecy of Israel's national restoration — the text itself says so explicitly (37:11-12: 'these bones are the whole house of Israel... I will open your graves and bring you into the land of Israel'). After the destruction of 586 BC, Israel was 'dead' as a nation; the vision promises national revival. Many religious Zionists see 1948 as a partial fulfillment. The Talmud also uses Ezekiel 37 as a proof text for physical resurrection (Sanhedrin 92b), debating whether the dry bones vision was literal (a historical miracle) or purely metaphorical. Full fulfillment awaits the messianic age, when all Israel will be restored under a Davidic king (37:24-25).

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Ezekiel 37:1-14 is one of the most vivid prophetic visions in the Hebrew Bible. The text itself provides the interpretation (vv.11-14): the bones = Israel in exile, the resurrection = national restoration. The secondary use as a proof text for individual bodily resurrection (as in the Talmud and in Christian theology) goes beyond the passage's stated meaning but is a legitimate extension of the imagery. The word 'ruach' in this passage means both 'wind,' 'breath,' and 'spirit' — Ezekiel deliberately exploits the ambiguity (v.9: 'Come from the four winds/spirits, O breath/spirit, and breathe on these slain'). This echoes Genesis 2:7 where God breathes into Adam — new creation language. The second half of the chapter (37:15-28, the two sticks becoming one) prophesies the reunification of Judah and Israel under one Davidic king.

prophecies.title: One Shepherd — My Servant David
prophecies.text_snippet: I will set over them one shepherd, My servant David, and he will feed them; he will feed them himself and be their shepherd. And I, the LORD, will be their God, and My servant David will be prince among them

christian_view.explanation: Jesus identified Himself as the 'Good Shepherd' (John 10:11), explicitly contrasting Himself with the 'hired hands' (false shepherds) that Ezekiel 34 condemns. He said 'I have other sheep that are not of this fold' (John 10:16), echoing the gathering theme. The 'one shepherd' and 'one flock' imagery points to Christ unifying Jew and Gentile. The reference to 'My servant David' identifies this shepherd as the Davidic Messiah, not David himself returned from the dead. Hebrews 13:20 calls Jesus 'the great Shepherd of the sheep.' The full visible shepherding of all God's people awaits the consummation.

jewish_view.explanation: This is a straightforward messianic prophecy in Jewish tradition: God will raise up a righteous king from David's line who will shepherd Israel — replacing the corrupt leaders (shepherds) who exploited the flock (Ezek 34:1-10). 'My servant David' is understood as a descendant of David, not a literally resurrected David. This Davidic king will unite Israel, rule justly, and dwell in the land forever (37:25). Since no Davidic king has fulfilled these conditions since the exile, the Messiah is still awaited. The imagery emphasizes the Messiah's role as a servant-leader, not a divine being — he is 'prince' (nasi) among the people, subordinate to God who says 'I, the LORD, will be their God' (34:24).

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The shepherd metaphor for kingship is pervasive in the ancient Near East — Hammurabi called himself 'the shepherd of the people,' and Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Hittite kings used the title. In Ezekiel 34, God first condemns the current shepherds (rulers), then says HE Himself will shepherd Israel (34:11-16), then says He will appoint 'My servant David' (34:23). This creates a theological tension: is the shepherd God or David? Christians resolve this by seeing Christ as both divine and Davidic. Jewish tradition resolves it by distinguishing: God shepherds through His appointed king. The title 'nasi' (prince, 34:24) rather than 'melek' (king) is deliberate — Ezekiel reserves 'melek' for God alone and uses 'nasi' for the human ruler, emphasizing subordination.

prophecies.title: The New Temple — Ezekiel's Visionary Temple
prophecies.text_snippet: The name of the city from that day shall be: The LORD Is There

christian_view.explanation: Christians are deeply divided on Ezekiel's temple vision. (1) Spiritual/symbolic: Jesus is the true Temple (John 2:19-21), the Church is the temple of God (1 Cor 3:16; Eph 2:19-22), and Revelation 21:22 says of the New Jerusalem 'I saw no temple in it, for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple.' Ezekiel's vision is fulfilled in Christ and the Church, not in a literal building. (2) Literal/dispensational: a real temple will be built in the millennial kingdom, with memorial sacrifices (not atoning ones). The extraordinary architectural detail (9 chapters) suggests a literal plan. (3) Idealized: the vision portrays the perfected worship-community without requiring exact literal fulfillment.

jewish_view.explanation: Orthodox Judaism takes the future Temple literally. The Third Temple will be built in the messianic age, either by the Messiah himself (Maimonides, Hilkhot Melakhim 11:1) or descending from heaven as a divine gift (Rashi on Sukkah 41a). Ezekiel 40-48 provides the blueprint. Sacrificial worship will be restored — this is a non-negotiable aspect of the messianic age in traditional Judaism. The daily Amidah prayer petitions: 'Restore the service to the inner sanctuary of Your Temple.' However, there are admitted difficulties: Ezekiel's temple dimensions differ from Solomon's and the Second Temple, and some ritual details conflict with the Torah's prescriptions (which the Talmud acknowledges but defers to Elijah's resolution — Menachot 45a).

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Ezekiel 40-48 is the longest sustained vision in the prophetic corpus. The temple's dimensions are given with extreme precision (cubits, gates, chambers), yet key elements differ from the Torah's prescriptions: the tribal allotments divide the land in parallel horizontal strips (not matching Joshua's boundaries), the river from the temple (47:1-12) has no historical precedent, and the prince's (nasi) role differs from the Levitical legislation. The Talmud (Menachot 45a; Shabbat 13b) notes the contradictions between Ezekiel and the Torah and reportedly considered excluding Ezekiel from the canon until Rabbi Hananiah ben Hezekiah spent 300 jars of lamp oil harmonizing them. The closing name of the city — 'YHWH Shammah' (The LORD is There) — emphasizes divine presence rather than architectural structure as the ultimate point.

prophecies.title: The Bethlehem Prophecy — Ruler from Ancient Days
prophecies.text_snippet: But as for you, Bethlehem Ephrathah, too little to be among the clans of Judah, from you One will go forth for Me to be ruler in Israel. His goings forth are from long ago, from the days of eternity

christian_view.explanation: Matthew 2:4-6 records that when Herod asked the chief priests where the Messiah would be born, they quoted Micah 5:2 without hesitation: Bethlehem. Jesus was born in Bethlehem (Luke 2:4-7). The phrase 'His goings forth are from long ago, from the days of eternity' (mimei olam) is understood as a reference to Christ's pre-existence and eternal divine nature — He did not begin to exist at His birth in Bethlehem. John 7:42 shows that the Jewish expectation of a Bethlehem-born Messiah was common knowledge. The juxtaposition of Bethlehem's smallness with the ruler's cosmic scope ('to the ends of the earth,' 5:4) points to the paradox of the incarnation.

jewish_view.explanation: Jewish tradition agrees this is a messianic prophecy pointing to Bethlehem as the Messiah's birthplace — the Targum Jonathan translates it messianically. The point is not Bethlehem the town but Bethlehem as the ancestral home of David's clan — the Messiah will be from David's family (the clan of Jesse/ Ephrathah). 'From the days of eternity' (mimei olam) means 'from ancient times' — the Davidic dynasty was established long ago, not that the Messiah is an eternal divine being. The same phrase ('mimei olam') is used elsewhere of ancient things, not necessarily of literal eternity (cf. Isa 63:9, 11; Amos 9:11; Mic 7:14). The Messiah has not yet come from Bethlehem to rule Israel and bring peace (5:4-5).

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The Hebrew 'motsa'otav mikedem mimei olam' is debated: 'mikedem' can mean 'from of old' or 'from the east' (geographic); 'mimei olam' can mean 'from days of eternity' (pointing to pre-existence) or 'from days of old' (pointing to the ancient Davidic line). The LXX translates with 'ap arches, ex hemeron aionos' (from the beginning, from days of the age) — ambiguous. The Targum reads: 'whose name was spoken from of old, from the days of eternity,' which could support either pre-existence of the person or pre-existence of the divine plan. Bethlehem Ephrathah is distinguished from Bethlehem in Zebulun (Josh 19:15). The connection to Ruth 4:11 (Ephrathah = the clan of Boaz and Jesse) grounds the prophecy in Davidic lineage.

prophecies.title: Swords into Plowshares — Universal Peace
prophecies.text_snippet: They will hammer their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks; nation will not lift up sword against nation, and never again will they train for war

christian_view.explanation: This vision (virtually identical to Isaiah 2:2-4) describes a future age when all nations stream to God's mountain to learn His ways, and war ceases entirely. Christians acknowledge this has not been fulfilled — it awaits either the millennial kingdom (premillennialism) or the eternal state (amillennialism). The Church age is marked by the 'already/not yet' tension: Christ has made peace between God and humanity (Col 1:20), but geopolitical peace awaits His return. Micah 4:4 adds the distinctive detail 'every man will sit under his vine and under his fig tree' — a picture of agrarian prosperity and security.

jewish_view.explanation: This is the definitive vision of the messianic age in Judaism: universal peace, nations learning Torah from Zion, the end of warfare. It is explicitly unfulfilled — and its unfulfillment is one of Judaism's primary arguments against the Christian claim that the Messiah has already come. Maimonides (Hilkhot Melakhim 12:1) uses this passage as the standard: in the messianic age, 'there will be no war, no famine, no jealousy, and no competition.' Since none of this has happened, the Messiah has not come. The passage is inscribed on the wall across from the United Nations building in New York — an acknowledgment of its unfulfilled aspiration by the nations themselves.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: Micah 4:1-3 is nearly identical to Isaiah 2:2-4. Scholars debate priority: did Micah borrow from Isaiah, Isaiah from Micah, or both from a common source? Since Micah and Isaiah were contemporaries (late 8th c. BC), any scenario is possible. Micah 4:4 (vine and fig tree) is unique to Micah and may represent the original form. The vision of universal pilgrimage to Zion reflects Jerusalem's role as the cosmic center (axis mundi) in Israelite theology. The 'mountain of the LORD' being 'raised above the hills' (4:1) may be literal (eschatological geography) or figurative (Zion's spiritual supremacy). The paradox: Torah goes forth from Zion to all nations, yet the nations come to Zion to receive it — both centripetal and centrifugal mission.

prophecies.title: The Triumphal Entry — King on a Donkey
prophecies.text_snippet: Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion! Shout in triumph, O daughter of Jerusalem! Behold, your king is coming to you; He is just and endowed with salvation, humble, and mounted on a donkey, even on a colt, the foal of a donkey

christian_view.explanation: All four Gospels record Jesus's deliberate fulfillment of this prophecy on Palm Sunday. Matthew 21:4-5 explicitly states 'This took place to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet.' Jesus sent disciples to find the donkey and colt, intentionally staging the entry according to Zechariah's prophecy. The crowds cried 'Hosanna' (from Ps 118:25-26), recognizing the messianic significance. The king comes 'humble' (ani) — not on a war horse but on a donkey, the mount of peace. He is 'just' (tsaddiq) and 'endowed with salvation' (nosha, passive: 'saved/victorious') — a victorious king who achieves victory through God, not military might.

jewish_view.explanation: This is recognized as messianic in rabbinic tradition. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 98a) famously discusses the apparent contradiction between Daniel 7:13 (Messiah coming with clouds — in glory) and Zechariah 9:9 (Messiah coming on a donkey — in humility). Rabbi Alexandri resolves: if Israel is worthy, the Messiah comes with clouds; if unworthy, humble on a donkey. This implies two MODES of coming, not two comings. A riding-a-donkey Messiah has not yet brought the peace described in v.10 ('He will speak peace to the nations; His dominion will be from sea to sea'). Jesus rode into Jerusalem on a donkey but did not establish peace or universal dominion — therefore, the prophecy remains unfulfilled.

prophecies.scholarly_notes: The Hebrew 'ani v'rokhev al chamor' is 'humble and riding on a donkey' — the donkey is not a sign of poverty but of PEACE. Kings rode horses in war and donkeys in peace (cf. Judges 5:10; 10:4; 2 Sam 16:2). Solomon rode David's mule to his coronation (1 Kings 1:33). The king arrives as a peacemaker, not a conqueror. Matthew 21:2 has Jesus requesting 'a donkey and a colt,' leading both animals in — possibly fulfilling the Hebrew poetic parallelism literally (donkey // colt of a donkey), though Mark and Luke mention only one animal. Zechariah 9:10 continues with 'I will cut off the chariot from Ephraim and the war horse from Jerusalem' — total disarmament under the Messiah's peace, clearly unfulfilled.

prophecies.title: The Pierced One — They Will Look on Me
prophecies.text_snippet: They will look on Me whom they have pierced; and they will mourn for Him, as one mourns for an only son, and they will weep bitterly over Him like the bitter weeping over a firstborn
